data.title: Asia 2026: 5 Questions For Japan's Year Of 'Sanaenomics'
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

What are the main reasons for economic recovery in 2026?

  • Japan’s economy is expected to recover quite meaningfully in 2026 thanks to 'Sanaenomics' and reduced trade tensions. A lower cost of living and solid wage growth are expected to support private spending, while easing trade tensions should boost exports. Combined with structural labour shortages at home, robust global AI demand, reduced US tariff uncertainty, and solid corporate earnings, we expect significant investment growth in 2026. For wage growth, Rengo, one of the largest trade unions in Japan, is vying for pay raises of at least 5% during the upcoming spring wage negotiations. We expect another near-5% wage growth rise for 2026, which will eventually turn real wages positive.
  • Like other major Asian tech exporters, strong demand for semiconductors is expected to drive export growth. In addition, following the US-Japan trade agreement, auto exports have started to recover. We believe the weak yen has helped offset some of the effects of tariffs, which will continue to benefit Japanese exporters.
  • The Bank of Japan’s slow and steady normalisation process will continue, but real interest rates will remain negative. Accommodative monetary conditions will therefore support growth.

Semiconductors will boost exports, and US exports should rebound

Will the BoJ continue to hike rates?

  • The BoJ continued its move toward policy normalisation in 2025, implementing a 25bp hike in the first quarter and pursuing quantitative tightening by reducing its holdings of Japanese government bonds (JGBs) and ETF/J-REITs. Given recent hawkish remarks from several BoJ officials, including Governor Kazuo Ueda, the likelihood of another rate hike in December appears high.
  • Growth conditions are projected to improve in 2026, with core inflation remaining above 2%. This may prompt the BoJ to consider a 25bp hike, with the policy rate reaching 1.0%. The BoJ will monitor whether wage growth remains above 3% in the spring negotiations and whether underlying inflation remains near 2%. Policy normalisation will likely proceed slowly due to policy coordination with the government. We expect the next hike to materialise much later, in October. With real interest rates still negative, a 25bp hike is justifiable in our view.
  • However, there is potential for the BoJ to raise rates to a total of 50bp, depending on wage growth and the strength of private consumption. This could be triggered, ironically, by Sanaenomics.

Core inflation excluding fresh food and energy will stay well above 2%, supporting the BoJ's rate hike

How might Sanaenomics affect the economy and monetary policy?

  • In early December, the Japanese cabinet approved the government’s first economic stimulus package, valued at approximately ¥21.3tr. The initiatives are focused on stabilising inflation, enhancing defence and diplomacy, and promoting sustainable growth.
  • We agree with the need for a stimulus package to boost short-term growth and reduce inflation, but this may influence the BoJ’s monetary policy.
  • Inflation is projected to drop below 2% when energy subsidies are introduced early next year, with an expected decrease to 1.4% year-on-year in 2026 from 3.1% in 2025. Lower energy and food costs, financial support for childcare, and a strong equity market should stimulate more consumer spending, which may result in stronger growth than anticipated. This may also push up core inflation above 2%.So far, BoJ policymakers have suggested that current inflation is mainly driven by cost-push factors, meaning the underlying rate of increase hasn't yet reached 2%. Should core inflation continue to exceed headline inflation, it would indicate demand-driven inflationary conditions. In turn, the BoJ may implement more rate hikes or introduce them earlier than anticipated.

Sanaenomics might boost growth more than expected

What might Sanaenomics mean for financial markets?

  • The recent sell-off in JGBs was influenced by multiple factors, including high odds for a BoJ rate hike in December, increased debt issuance thanks to stimulus packages, and the possible unwinding of carry trades. As is widely known, Sanaenomics is based on aggressive fiscal spending to revitalise the economy. However, combined with the ongoing BoJ’s quantitative tightening, market concerns about fiscal sustainability could increase. This could boost JGB yields and weaken the JPY at the same time.
  • If yields on short-term JGBs increase rapidly, it could place a strain on households and businesses. Long-term JGBs are primarily traded by institutional investors and have a limited direct connection to the real economy. However, any sudden surge in short-term JGB yields might negatively impact the fragile economic recovery.
  • If the economy experiences a downturn, the BoJ may encounter difficulties. A sudden policy change could increase market volatility and damage overall conditions. As a result, this might prevent the BoJ from responding promptly to changes in macroeconomic conditions.

Will tensions between Japan and China increase?

  • Other key aspects of Sanaenomics include strengthening defence and diplomatic capabilities. If not implemented carefully, this policy may lead to increased regional tensions, as observed between Japan and China. We don’t think the tensions will escalate further for now. Since her conversation with US President Donald Trump, Takaichi has not commented further on China or Taiwan, suggesting the status quo will hold. With strong approval ratings, Takaichi has little incentive to provoke China.
  • Any deterioration in relations would affect Japan more than China. For example, after Japan purchased the disputed Senkaku Islands in 2012, tensions led to a 40% drop in Chinese tourism to Japan. This time, the Chinese government has cancelled flights and banned Japanese seafood in retaliation. While it's hard to gauge the full impact on Japan's economy, these actions pose clear short-term downside risks.

We expect to see a decline of Chinese tourists to Japan

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: What OpenAI's 'Code Red' Means For The Markets
data.description:
One of the biggest stories so far in December is Sam Altman, the CEO of OpenAI, just declaring a 'code red,' which has gotten outsized coverage in the financial press. It was his call to get the company more intently refocused on improving the capabilities of ChatGPT given slowing subscriptions, intensifying competition, as well as surging investment costs.

Since the launch of ChatGPT almost exactly three years ago, OpenAI has become one of the fastest-growing commercial concerns in the world, with revenues currently tracking to a $20 billion annual run rate. More than 800 million people around the globe use ChatGPT on a daily basis.

However, the company is facing growing competition from the likes of Alphabet (GOOG) and Anthropic, which may beat OpenAI on the IPO front. In late October, Microsoft (MSFT) and OpenAI announced a restructuring deal that now allows OpenAI to transition away from its nonprofit roots and likely go public. Mr. Altman just declared OpenAI will now be pulling back on investments in areas like health, shopping, and advertising. It will now be more intently focused on prioritizing improving ChatGPT capabilities and will be having daily calls around this effort.

This makes sense as Claude chatbot maker Anthropic continues to make inroads in the business community, and the recent release of Google’s Gemini 3 model last month has helped it add over 200 million users over the past three months. Gemini 3 has also outperformed ChatGPT on some industry benchmark tests. The new upgrade has garnered notable praise and buzz on social media as well. Of note, Gemini crossed 450 million monthly active users in July of this year. ChatGPT is no longer the only game in town.

OpenAI's challenges, if not addressed successfully, could undermine the entire AI Narrative and ecosystem. This is critical to the market, as most of the equity gains over the past three years have been driven by AI-related megacaps like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Microsoft. The Magnificent Seven now accounts for nearly 40% of overall market capitalization.

If OpenAI growth becomes challenged in any way, it could become a massive headwind for the overall market. That is because OpenAI is one of the key 'jenga' pieces within the Vendor-Financing-Circle that is akin to the vendor financing offered by the likes of Cisco Networks (CSCO), Lucent, and others near the end of the Internet Boom a quarter century ago. But on steroids.

Despite bleeding cash and with approximate revenues of $14 billion in FY2025, OpenAI has made just over $1.4 trillion in commitments over the next eight years for future computing power. Fortune recently projected that OpenAI will not even be profitable in FY2030 and will have to come up with over $200 billion in additional capital over that time to fund current commitments. OpenAI's capital budget is expected to grow to $17 million in FY2026, $35 billion in FY2027, and $45 billion in FY2028.

If the funding spigot dries up for any reason, numerous major players in the AI ecosystem could take major hits. Some of the most significant agreements that OpenAI has in place are highlighted below. During the summer, OpenAI and NVIDIA announced a massive AI partnership.

However, NVIDIA's CFO just admitted this 'deal' is still at the 'letter of intent' stage. In mid-September, OpenAI struck a deal with Oracle (ORCL) to purchase $300 billion in computing power over five years. This is capacity that Oracle does not currently have but is in the process of building, partially financed by debt. In fact, Meta Platforms (META), Google, Amazon, and Oracle have added just over $200 billion in debt so far in 2025, which includes off-balance sheet financing. This is more than these four entities have added in debt over the past seven years combined, to put it in perspective.

Finally, one month ago, OpenAI inked a seven-year, $38 billion deal to buy cloud services from Amazon (AMZN).

OpenAI is hardly the only thing true believers in the AI Revolution need to worry about. Based on current projections, the tech giants driving the AI Revolution will have accumulated $2.5 trillion of AI-related assets by 2030. On a five-year depreciation schedule, this would mean $500 billion in annual depreciation cost. This would be more than the combined profits of these AI juggernauts in FY2025. What possibly could go wrong? And that is without getting into whether AI GPUs will actually have a five-year useful life, instead of the historical two to three years of previous chip generations before having to be replaced.

OpenAI's 'code red' is just the latest AI development prudent investors need to keep a close eye on. Anything that disrupts, postpones, or cancels OpenAI's future commitments could undermine the entire AI Narrative.

Given current valuation levels, this also could be a huge negative for the overall market as we head into 2026.

data.title: Beyond The Hype: The Silent Stability Beneath Market Speculation
data.description:
Markets have come under pressure lately as investors worry about potential overvaluation of AI investment returns and other speculative investments. Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist with TD Wealth, says the fundamental market drivers remain intact and could reward diversified investors.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: Markets have been choppy in recent trading. But according to our featured guest today, what we're seeing may not actually be a correction. Joining us now to discuss is Brad Simpson, Chief Market Strategist with TD Wealth.

And Brad, welcome to the show.

Brad Simpson: Great to be here.

Anthony Okolie Well, as I mentioned, we've seen a lot of volatility in markets recently. But you say that this is not a correction, but more of an unwinding. What's been happening there?

Brad Simpson: Yeah, let's start with, I think, we look into your introduction. If you axed out Canada, as soon as you went to the United States, you went to technology, update in technology, update in NASDAQ, update in technology names. And if you look at the correction that we've had here, if we back up a little bit and look at what it is I and my team do at the investment office here at TD Wealth, is we are the purveyors of building and managing diversified investment portfolios.

So, in the last few weeks, I was kind of sitting back and going, there's so much discussion around correction. I don't see a correction. I don't see them across our models. I don't see them across our portfolios. And what gives?

And so, I really started to dig into it, and of course I see the technology and the technology movement. But the reality is, this unwinding we're talking about is what I would call a massive retail unwind of the technology trade. And folks that are overlevered, speculators into Bitcoin and anything that is an AI or an AI beneficiary, that's what we're seeing.

If we were looking at what this correction is not, is would be, first of all, we don't have any early signs of a recession going on right now. We don't have a break in liquidity-- there's lots of it inside the market. We're not in the middle of a credit crisis. We are not in the middle of seeing a reversal of CapEx spending. And ultimately, as we have seen the earnings season we just went through, we don't have deteriorating earnings either. In fact, we're in an incredibly buoyant time for earnings.

So I think if you look at it in those terms, is that what we're seeing is-- again, to hit this home-- is there is this speculative phase that's going on in this market, and this speculative phase is folks are discovering that if you're really using a lot of leverage, and you're chasing one sector of the market, and there's a lot of froth in that market, the net result is-- and I think we're looking up here. We're looking at here the run-up in Bitcoin and the run-up of-- we're looking at a NASDAQ. So NASDAQ, 3 times levered NASDAQ, and looking at the run-up and looking at the drop-off here.

And on the bottom line, we're looking at the S&P 500. And we're looking at margin debt and margin calls. And all of that is, I would argue, is first and foremost, the good news is it's very orderly, and it's in a concentrated part of the market. But I think you certainly, certainly could not call this a major market correction in any way, shape, or form.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. Now, you identified 2 stabilizers there for the markets: liquidity and earnings power. Are they still intact?

Brad Simpson: Yeah. Look, the-- and I should probably say that on my way over here, we've published our monthly perspectives, and our monthly perspectives is called Gravity Always Wins. And much of what we're hearing is coming from there. And so, for folks who want to read it early, when it comes out you can go to my LinkedIn, and you'll find it there.

And so, if you look at it at an environment like this one, what ends a market and what ultimately leads to a market correction is either two things. One is you look at liquidity in the system. And when we look at the system today, there's ample liquidity. In fact, I think if you went back over the last couple of months, and if you said inside the major part of the indices, where we're seeing the hyperscalers go through a correction, you could actually argue it's been a very orderly correction that we're going through there.

And the starting point to that is that we can look at something in the central bank in the US and look at their net margin levels. And if we look at the net margin levels in going through September, October, and November, money was tightening up inside of there. And as that money has tightened up a little bit, we saw a little bit of a price correction in the overall market as a whole.

When we look forward to going into December, we know all the discussion around the central banks and the US saying that there may be a drop of interest rates by 25 basis points in December. Even if there's not, they've well telegraphed there's three coming, either in December-- one in December, and then two more next year or three next year. And they've also been telegraphing that they're going to be loosening up on the quantitative tightening.

All of that points to more liquidity coming. And I think if you look at the massive fiscal spend that is out there in the US, if we go into 2026 and when we start seeing those tax cuts from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act start to come out, ample liquidity in the system and more of it coming.

And then, on the other side, when we look at earnings growth, if you look at the S&P 500 for 2026, roughly you're looking at earnings expectations of let's call it somewhere around 13%. You go over into Canada, you're somewhere around 10%. You go into Europe, you're somewhere between 8% and 9%. That's a really good, consistent earnings story that we're looking and talking about there.

And I think that as long as there's ample liquidity in the system, and on the other side, as long as there's really, really healthy earnings growth, that the likelihood of a big correction or of this market going through something devious or ugly is rather low. And I think it's important that we keep being mindful of that.

Anthony Okolie: When you talk about volatility in the markets, as you mentioned, explain to us how the rise of quant funds as well as retail investors has been driving that market that we've seen this year.

Brad Simpson: Yeah, look, that-- I don't want to lay this whole correction on the footstep of retail trading. But boy, you've got to say, if it's not the catalyst to it, it's been the facilitator of a bigger rush down.

So what we're looking at here in this chart kind of hits it all home. We have gone through a massive shift on what make markets tick over the last 4 years. So we're looking here is the investors in retail, high-frequency quant funds, and fundamental investors.

So if you look over at that 2010, how much of the market was made up of fundamental investors, look over to your far right and how much of that is now made up of fundamental investors, go back to 2010, and look at the difference between retail and high-frequency quant, and look over on the other side there.

So, I'll give you some hard numbers on this. Going into COVID, triple-levered ETF mandates or products that you could allocate to was roughly $45 billion. Fast forward today, it's about $250 billion. That $250 billion swings in the market every day. And what it's doing is, it's disproportionately having retail swing inside this market. And what we're looking here on the screen here is, (a) it's having that impact.

What we're looking at here is we're showing that liquidity flows and the GS retail favorites basket. And that is basically retail favorites basket of a lot of either tech-oriented names or companies that actually have pretty weak balance sheets but have momentum behind them and leverage behind them, and some sort of reference towards AI one way or the other, or Bitcoin-- either of those two things. And so, we see that liquidity push on it.

What we're looking there is, that's that Fed asset number I was talking about. You start to see it decline. And you start to see the retail favorites start to come off. So, retail and this absolute massive change that we've had through the use of levered products and platforms and direct platforms and all of those things have had a significant impact.

And now what we're seeing is, think about your average-- I admit, I'm a massive sports fan. I never miss a Raptors game. And every single time when I'm watching a Raptors game, and a gambling-- every five minutes, a gambling thing comes on. I must admit, I black out. I try to ignore it. The intersection between markets and gambling-- right now, we see the Commodity Exchange and negotiations, how they can link up and do business with FanDuel.

Anthony Okolie: It seems that the lines between investing and betting are blurring.

Brad Simpson: Absolutely, they are. And that is all kind of coming to the push of what we're seeing today, is that this intersection of these two are starting to have an impact.

Anthony Okolie: Is that what's driving that speculation that you talked about, and that volatility?

Brad Simpson: I think the driver between it ultimately is, one, is you know has been that as we've left COVID, we kept the foot on the pedal on keeping liquidity out there. Liquidity, as we just talked about, is one of the main drivers, right? Then you click in and you get all this new era talk, all this stuff about AI and the difference in there. Then you throw in this kind of Bitcoin story and you get those mix. So all of a sudden, you get an awful lot of people either saying, look, why should I work? Why do I need a steady paycheck? I can stay at home. There's very low volatility on this, and I can trade these things all day.

So we've seen this massive change in movement inside of the market. And I think if you add up all those pieces, that's very symbolic of the market that we have today. And so when we sat back and we looked at it is that I think, and what an awful lot of folks are discovering right now-- it's back to the article we published today, is Gravity Always Wins-- is that when leverage comes into the picture and it starts to go against you, what happens is that you start seeing a market-- and right now, it's a narrow part of the market-- where, one, liquidity starts to dry up. Two is that everybody says, well, geez, I got to sell. Everybody starts to move for the exit at the same time.

And I think that's what we're in the middle of a little bit here, anyways, in what I would call the AI beneficiaries coming to the end in the major market, but in the speculative event of this, has been a major and significant factor. We've seen roughly somewhere between $2 and $2.5 trillion wiped out in this part of the market. That's no small sum.

Anthony Okolie: Okay, so with that backdrop, what should investors be thinking about or doing right now? What should they be keeping at the top of mind right now?

Brad Simpson: Well, I think that the starting point is that, I think you can see you've got to step back a little bit. And I think you have to say and understand the difference between speculating and investing. And I'm not making this sound like speculation is all bad. It's not. If you look at-- we've been writing an awful lot about that. We think that artificial intelligence that we're in the middle of, this is the fourth industrial revolution we're in the middle of. This is a really significant period of time.

If we went back in time, if we looked at the internet build-out, if we looked at it-- let's call it in the '90s-- if we looked at the television build-out in the '20s, if we looked at the railroad build-out of the 19th century, all of those had speculation involved in it and helped build an incredible amount of infrastructure and the systems that you and I benefit every day on.

So, I'm not saying, oh, all speculation is bad. What I'm saying is that confusing those two when you're an investor is that-- Typically I think what you need to do is take your net worth, put it on one piece of paper and then look at that and go, what is the money that I'm using that are for my long-term objectives? And what is the money that-- and then the other part of my money is going, what is what I call the bag of chips money, the money that if I lost it all, I'm all good with that?

And I think if you do those two things, clearly delineate the difference between those two-- don't kid yourself that, you know, being levered long triple Qs is an investment, but it's a speculation on a tech side of a market. And then understanding over here on your positioning and portfolio positioning.

There, create a wealth plan for yourself. Say, what is the cash needs that I immediately need to fund? What is the things that I want to invest in so that I can reduce the taxes and how the income that I'm deriving from my investments? What part is the investments am I using for the stuff I want to invest in five years from now? And then what is the stuff that I want to make sure that I'm investing for 10 years and longer out? And I think if you do those pieces, you're in really good shape.

Original Post

data.title: Royal Bank of Canada: Stellar Results, Limited Upside
data.description:
Thesis

Royal Bank of Canada (RY) is the largest bank in Canada and operates around the world in areas like personal banking, business banking, wealth management, insurance, and capital markets. When I last wrote about RBC in late August, I rated the stock as a Hold. The company’s core business looked very strong, with high returns on equity and solid capital levels. But the stock price already seemed expensive, and I didn’t see much room for it to go higher, especially since the benefits from the HSBC Canada deal and higher interest rates were starting to fade, while upcoming mortgage renewals and rising credit costs were becoming bigger concerns.

Since my call, RY has produced a total return of about 6.7%, which is just slightly better than the roughly 5.0% gain from the S&P 500 (SP500). Basically, the stock has mostly behaved the way I expected, but it's also broken out from a technical standpoint.

Keep in mind, I do recognize that the year-to-date results are strong, and the positive momentum is definitely working in RY’s favor.

That said, with the fourth-quarter results now out, my research shows that Royal Bank of Canada is operating at a very high level. The bank is delivering record profitability, has strong capital, and is showing real momentum in both Wealth Management and Capital Markets. But at the same time, the stock’s valuation already reflects most of that strength, leaving limited room for additional upside.

Royal Bank of Canada Performance Highlights

Royal Bank of Canada delivered one of the strongest years in its history. For the full year of 2025, it earned net income of $20.4 billion. That $20.4 billion was up 25% from the prior year. If you spread that profit across all the shares, you get diluted EPS of $14.07, also up 25%. Meanwhile, adjusted net income rose 20% to $20.9 billion and adjusted EPS increased 19% to $14.43 with return on equity moving up to 16.3% from 14.4%. And at the same time, the CET1 ratio, which is a key safety cushion regulators watch, stayed at 13.5%.

That level indicates the bank is still very well capitalized, even after returning $11.3 billion to shareholders through dividends and buybacks (see historical stats above).

Pre-provision, pre-tax earnings jumped 30% to $30 billion, the clearest evidence yet that the core earnings engine is firing on all cylinders. Higher net interest income came from solid Canadian banking volume growth and persistently attractive spreads, while fee-based revenue surged in Wealth Management on the back of strong market appreciation and continued net sales. Capital Markets also contributed meaningfully, with Global Markets and Corporate & Investment Banking both posting sharp revenue gains. The HSBC Canada deal folded in five extra months of results and is now fully absorbed, delivering the revenue bump they said it would without turning the cost base into a brushfire.

Every segment posted double-digit earnings growth except Commercial Banking, which still turned in a solid 7% despite eating higher provisions tied to rising unemployment, slower growth, and trade disruptions. Personal Banking was up 20%, Commercial 7%, Wealth Management 25%, Insurance 14%, and Capital Markets 18%.

The fourth quarter itself was a record-breaker. The bank earned $5.4 billion in net income and reported EPS of $3.76, with both numbers up 29% from a year earlier. Adjusted results were also strong, rising 25%.

For the full year, total revenue hit $66.6 billion, which is a 16% increase on a reported basis. Expenses went up too, but by a much smaller 7%, which helped create strong positive operating leverage.

Credit costs were higher, which is pretty normal at this stage of the economic cycle. For the full year, provisions for credit losses (PCL) came to $4.4 billion. The impaired loan ratio was 37 basis points, which is 9 basis points higher than last year, mainly due to issues in a few commercial industries.

Gross impaired loans increased to 83 basis points of total loans, up from 59 basis points, but the bank’s loan-loss reserves remain strong, and the extra provisions on still-performing loans were relatively small.

Liquidity remains extremely strong, with the Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) at 127%, giving the bank a large safety cushion. Royal Bank of Canada also raised its quarterly dividend by 6%, bringing it to $1.64. More importantly, it increased its medium-term return-on-equity (ROE) goal to 17% or higher for fiscal 2026.

Royal Bank of Canada Earnings Breakdown

What really jumps out to me in this report is how evenly Royal Bank of Canada’s earnings machine is running now. We saw that not every division, Personal Banking, Commercial Banking, Wealth Management, Insurance, and Capital Markets, is growing at the same speed, even so, together they’ve pushed the bank’s pre-provision, pre-tax earnings up 30% and they did it without relying on one big risky bet.

Personal Banking earnings were also up double digits, and Commercial Banking was lagging but still up. That’s pretty solid, especially considering OSFI has been warning again and again about higher unemployment, the risk of people renewing mortgages at tougher rates, and ongoing stress in commercial real estate across the system. In that environment, the fact that Canadian retail lending and commercial lending can still grow revenue while taking higher provisions for potential loan losses suggests the bank is being careful. It is not just pushing out loans to chase growth at any price.

To me, the bigger change is how much more important fee-based and market-related businesses have become for the bank. Wealth Management earnings rose 25%, helped by increases in fee-based client assets and strong net sales. RBC now oversees about $1.57 trillion in assets under management and roughly $5.6 trillion in assets under administration, both growing by double-digit percentages compared to last year.

Essentially, that positions the bank firmly in the global competition for asset and wealth management at a time when money continues to pour into ETFs and managed investment products. The U.S. ETF market alone is around $12.7 trillion in assets and has taken in about $950 billion in inflows so far this year. In practical terms, as that pool grows, a bank like Royal Bank of Canada can earn more steady fee income just by keeping and growing its share of client assets instead of relying only on interest spreads.

Capital Markets is, in my opinion, the other obvious growth driver. Full-year earnings for the segment were up 18%, and in Q4 alone, Capital Markets' net income surged more than 45% from a year earlier, thanks to stronger trading results and a comeback in M&A and underwriting activity. That matches what’s happening across the global investment-banking world, where fees are recovering, and advisory and equity capital markets revenues are growing at low double-digit rates as deal activity starts to pick up again.

RY’s Valuation

RY currently trades at 15.9× trailing non-GAAP earnings and 15.4× forward earnings, which is a 39% premium to the sector’s median valuation.

Its price-to-book ratio of 2.43× is even more extreme, about double that of its peers.

Seeking Alpha gives the stock a C- overall valuation grade, with D-level grades on almost every valuation metric except one: price-to-cash-flow, where it earns an A because the bank trades at only 3.45× cash flow. That looks cheap because banks throw off oceans of cash.

Analysts expect the P/E to come down to 14.4× in 2026 and 12.9× in 2027, based on modest 7.5–11% EPS growth.

Meanwhile, according to FAST Graphs' forecasting data, the fair value price target of C$216 basically implies no upside from where the stock trades today.

The valuation basically reflects a direct bet that management will hit its 17%+ return-on-equity target next year and keep it there. The HSBC acquisition costs are now fully absorbed, the bank’s AI tools are scaling, the business mix is shifting further toward high-margin Wealth and Capital Markets, and expense growth remains under control.

All of that seems reasonable, maybe even likely, as long as the Canadian economy keeps stumbling forward without slipping into a full recession.

But the safety cushion here is extremely thin. Revenue growth is expected to be only 5.4% going forward, compared to 7.2% for the sector, and net interest margins look set to dip again. Unemployment is still creeping up, and those commercial credit problems in autos and services probably aren’t going away anytime soon.

It would take just one quarter of stubbornly high expenses or one meaningful credit hit for the stock to get pushed back down to its old 12–13× earnings range. At this valuation, RBC gets zero credit for being the biggest Canadian bank.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’m sticking with my Hold rating on RY. The bank is performing extremely well, with real momentum in Wealth Management and Capital Markets, but at 15.9× trailing and 15.4× forward earnings, paired with only about 5.4% expected revenue growth and obvious credit and economic risks, I don’t see enough upside to justify paying such a high premium.

data.title: BBVA: The Risk-Reward Proposition Is Not Good For Long-Term Investors
data.description:
While I was bearish on Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria, S.A. (BBVA) about one year ago due to its sensitivity to rates, this has not played out as I was expecting, as the bank’s share price has more than doubled since then. The market has rewarded European banks for their somewhat aggressive capital return plans in recent quarters, and BBVA was no exception. Additionally, its failed attempt to buy its smaller Spanish competitor Banco Sabadell (OTCPK:BNDSY) was also well received by investors because it’s more likely that BBVA will return more capital to shareholders rather than seeking external growth in the near future.

Regarding its takeover offer for Banco Sabadell, after a relatively long period of regulatory review that took more than one year, the bank got approval to go ahead with its offer some months ago, but BBVA could not get the backing from most of Sabadell’s shareholders. Indeed, only about 25% of Sabadell’s shareholders accepted the offer, being below the 30% threshold required by the Spanish regulator. As the bank was not able to get at least 30%, the offer was automatically cancelled, and BBVA is not allowed to make a new offer for at least one year.

As BBVA is usually quite conservative regarding its M&A activity, I think it’s not much likely that BBVA will try again in the near future to buy Sabadell, but eventually, it may pursue a new takeover approach in the medium term if the opportunity arises and the price is attractive. Investors should note that BBVA had some talks about a potential acquisition back in 2020, but decided to walk away due to valuation issues, making the most recent attempt the second time it failed to acquire its smaller competitor.

As the Spanish banking market has become more concentrated over the past decade and there are nowadays a lower number of banks compared to ten or fifteen years ago, Sabadell is the most obvious target for a larger bank like BBVA or Banco Santander (SAN). This means BBVA could potentially try again to buy Sabadell in the future, but following this failed attempt, the bank announced a new share buyback program of €1 billion and announced an interim dividend of €0.32 per share, representing an increase of more than 10% from the previous interim dividend. This clearly shows that BBVA’s strategy has changed again to reward its shareholders instead of pursuing external growth, something that it’s not much likely to change in the near term.

From an income perspective, BBVA has distributed two dividends over the past year, for a total amount of €0.73 per share, leading to a dividend yield at its current share price of about 3.9%. This yield is much lower than it was in the past (it was yielding around 7% when I last analyzed it) due to a rising share price in recent years, making it less attractive from an income perspective than before. This is now below the European banking sector average dividend yield, which is around 4.9%, and also lower than compared to some of its peers, including Sabadell or Caixabank (OTCPK:CAIXY), showing that BBVA’s income appeal is not particularly impressive right now.

However, I think it’s quite likely that BBVA will increase its final dividend related to 2025 earnings by double-digits, as the bank’s operating income continues to show some resilience and the bank is well capitalized, allowing it to maintain an attractive capital return policy to shareholders. As can be seen in the next graph, BBVA’s CET1 ratio was 13.4% at the end of last September, being well above both its capital requirement and internal target range.

This means BBVA has an excess capital position and can distribute the vast majority of its earnings to shareholders, as the bank does not need to build up its capital position. Therefore, it’s not surprising to see that current estimates are for BBVA to distribute an annual dividend related to 2025 earnings of €0.82 per share, implying a final dividend of €0.50 per share. This represents an increase of 22% compared to its final dividend of €0.41 per share related to 2024 earnings, showing that BBVA is clearly committed to returning more capital to shareholders following its failed takeover attempt on Sabadell. At its current share price, this leads to a forward-dividend yield of about 4.35%, being nonetheless below average compared to other European banks and its closest peers. Its dividend payout ratio is expected to be less than 50%, which is clearly conservative and shows that BBVA’s dividend is sustainable over the long term.

From an operating perspective, as I was expecting when I last analyzed BBVA, the bank’s earnings have peaked in recent quarters and are now showing some weakness, a trend that is justified by its business model, that is considerably exposed to rates. Indeed, its net attributable profit was slightly above €2.5 billion during the third quarter of 2025 (Q3 2025), representing a quarterly decline of 8% and a drop of 3.7% on an annual basis.

This negative performance is justified by lower rates in Europe and Mexico over the past year, being a headwind to net interest income (NII), and also by higher operating expenses. BBVA is one of the European banks with a higher reliance on NII on total revenues, which was a major reason I was cautious about its financial performance at the end of last year. However, due to strong loan growth, the bank has been able to increase its NII in recent quarters, a better performance than I was expecting.

In Q3 2025, BBVA’s NII amounted to €6.6 billion, up by 13.2% YoY, a very good performance despite lower rates in Spain and Mexico. This was driven by strong activity and loan growth of 16% YoY, which was key to offset rate headwinds over the last year. Nevertheless, customer spreads were negatively affected in both countries, with a bigger impact in Spain as a large part of loans are set at variable rates.

However, interest rates are expected to be near or at the terminal rates in both geographies, which means pressure on customer spreads should ease ahead and, potentially, customer spreads may reverse over the coming quarters, supported by loan growth.

Regarding expenses, BBVA reported somewhat high expense growth in recent quarters, especially in Mexico and Turkey, due to inflationary pressures. This has pushed cost growth to 7.3% YoY in Q3 2025, being a higher rate than revenue growth and resulting in negative jaws, not being a positive development for the bank’s efficiency. Indeed, its cost-to-income ratio was close to 40% during the past quarter, while its target is to achieve a cost-to-income ratio around 35% in the medium term, something that may be achievable if loan growth continues strongly ahead and the bank can contain cost growth to moderate levels over the coming years.

Regarding asset quality, while there has been some deterioration in recent quarters, the bank’s overall credit quality remains solid and not much different from the recent past. This has been a better development than I was expecting some months ago, as the credit quality in the banking system has remained resilient despite several external shocks for BBVA, such as the U.S. trade war or economic instability in Turkey. As shown in the next graph, its cost of risk ratio is much higher than compared to other European banks, which usually report cost of risk ratios below 0.5%, but it’s closer to its peer Banco Santander and remains at acceptable levels for a bank that has a large exposure to emerging markets.

While its net profit was down in the last quarter, and earnings growth may be relatively low ahead due to cost pressures that may offset some revenue growth driven by its loan book expansion, the market continues to focus on capital returns and has pushed banking valuations to relatively high levels. BBVA has been no exception and is currently trading at 1.85x book value, being the highest valuation over the last decade by a wide margin, as shown in the next graph.

This shows that BBVA’s strong share price performance in recent months has been driven by a re-rating of its shares rather than being supported by earnings growth, something that I don’t see as sustainable for long. Investors should keep in mind that the banking sector is cyclical and valuations are, historically, mean reverting throughout the economic cycle. This means that current high P/BV multiples may not be sustainable if economic conditions deteriorate ahead. Moreover, this valuation is also clearly at a premium to its peers, given that the sector’s average is only around 1.2x book value, being another sign of overvaluation.

Conclusion

BBVA’s strong share price rally in recent months has been driven by a higher valuation multiple, pushing its P/BV multiple to levels that I was not clearly expecting a year ago. While the bank’s operating performance has been better than I was expecting and credit losses have remained at acceptable levels, the bank is not reporting earnings growth, and this is not likely to change much in the near term, making me cautious about the sustainability of its valuation. In my opinion, the risk-reward proposition for long-term investors is not great, and its income appeal is also lower than it was in the past, and, therefore, I maintain my ‘sell’ recommendation as I see its valuation has been too high compared to its own history and its peers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Okta Q3: I'm Giving Up On This Company (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

At the end of 2024, I had picked Okta (OKTA) as my cybersecurity pick for 2025, with a potential 60% upside.

When most of the gains were realized by May, I downgraded the stock to a “hold.” Note that for the first 5 months of 2025, Okta was indeed the best-performing cybersecurity stock.

Unfortunately, investor enthusiasm quickly faded after its Q1 FY26 earnings and failed to revive after that.

With the stock declining after my May downgrade, I had upgraded my rating to a “buy” at the end of June and once again in September. Unfortunately, in both instances, I was wrong, with the stock underperforming the S&P 500. Despite being the best cybersecurity performer until May, it is now the worst-performing, which is quite disappointing in my opinion.

Okta reported its Q3 FY26 earnings yesterday, and despite a beat on both revenue and earnings estimates, forward guidance was quite unimpressive, with cRPO (Current Remaining Performance Obligations) projected to slow down 400 basis points next quarter. While management is optimistic about industry tailwinds around securing agents and non-human identities and their product innovation roadmap, the lack of a revenue reacceleration is quite problematic, especially when their competitor CyberArk (CYBR) is growing its revenues at least twice the rate.

As a result, I will downgrade my rating on Okta at this time to a “hold.” While there could be short-term trading opportunities, I don’t believe that the company has a long-term investment moat, at least not until we see clear evidence of a meaningful revenue turnaround.

Revenue and Earnings Beat Expectations Once Again

Okta just reported its Q3 FY26 earnings, where it beat both its revenue and earnings estimates by 1.58% and 8.38%, respectively.

On the revenue front, management had guided for revenue to grow between 9-10% YoY during Q3 with a non-GAAP operating margin of 22%. It turned out that Okta managed to grow much faster than management’s expectation, with their revenues growing 12% YoY and their non-GAAP operating margin coming in at 24%. Meanwhile, cRPO, which is a leading indicator of forward subscription revenues, also exceeded estimates, growing at 13% YoY, against management’s guidance of 10% YoY growth rates.

The strength in outperformance came from their steady growth in the $100K+ ACV (Annual Contract Value) customers, which grew 7% YoY (unchanged from the previous quarter) to a count of 5030.

During the earnings call, management discussed that the emergence of agentic technologies is redefining the “identity” security landscape, where securing AI agents and nonhuman identities is essential for large-scale enterprise AI adoption and deployment. As management pointed out, they have experienced “a surge in inbound interest for their Agentic Security solutions to manage agents,” where customers are looking for a “single control plane” to observe and manage agents.

As a result, Okta has been doubling down on their product innovation roadmap with new products that include Okta Identity Governance, Okta Privilege Access, Identity Security Posture Management, Identity Threat Protection with Okta AI, Okta Device Access, and Fine-Grained Authorization that are delivered as part of the Okta Workforce Identity Platform and are driving “meaningful contributions” to their top line, according to management. Separately, they also launched Auth0 for AI agents as part of their Customer Identity Cloud during the quarter that will allow customers to effortlessly build secure AI agents across their B2B, B2C, and internal app ecosystems.

To reiterate, Okta offers two primary platforms that include Okta Workforce and Okta Customer Identity that are powered by Auth0 technology. Okta Workforce is directed towards large enterprises and IT teams with use cases that include employee access, workflows, and compliance across cloud and on-premise applications. Meanwhile, the Okta Customer Identity platform powered by Auth0 is directed towards developers and product teams that are building user/customer applications that require a secure and customizable authentication and authorization experience for their users who are accessing the service.

Unimpressive Guidance, Growing Much Slower Compared to CyberArk

Despite an impressive track record of consistently beating both revenue and earnings estimates for two years in a row, the stock continues to struggle, and the price remains range-bound.

The main reason is tied to the lack of meaningful revenue reacceleration in Okta. Although the management is optimistic about industry tailwinds pertaining to AI Security and the investments they are making towards their product innovation, it is not yet reflected in the forward guidance. As a result, investor sentiment continues to remain dampened in the stock.

For the full year FY26, management raised their revenue guidance from their previous range of 10-11% to 11%. That is not impressive in my opinion. Moreover, looking forward, consensus estimates are pointing to a revenue deceleration in the high single-digit range over the next two years.

On the contrary, Okta’s nearest competitor, CyberArk (which Palo Alto Networks (PANW) has agreed to acquire, pending approval), is projected to grow its revenues at more than twice the rate of Okta’s over the next two years. Naturally, CyberArk stock has outperformed Okta stock as a result by over 10x, as can be seen below.

What’s even worse is that Okta management projected for cRPO growth to slow to 9% next quarter in Q4, down 400 basis points sequentially. While Okta’s management has a history of providing conservative guidance and then beating it, investors are now demanding a bold new path forward in order for the stock to break out of its trading range.

Revisiting My Valuation

There are two scenarios that I would like to discuss here.

Let’s discuss the bull case first.

In the bull scenario, I will assume that full-year FY26 revenues will grow at 11% per management guidance and will continue to grow at a similar range over the next two years to a total of $3.56B by FY28. Note, this is a faster growth rate than consensus estimates. This will be possible if Okta can start to translate revenue growth from its AI security products at a slightly faster rate, especially given the industry tailwinds. Assuming that non-GAAP operating margins grow by 100 basis points sequentially every year during the next two years, we will arrive at $1B in non-GAAP operating income.

The stock is currently trading at a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of close to 24, and assuming that price multiple remains anchored, we will arrive at a price target of $109 per share, representing an upside of 36% from its current levels.

Now, let’s move to the base case scenario.

This is where Okta grows its revenues by 11% YoY in FY26 as per management guidance and then grows at par with consensus estimates in the high single-digit range over the next two years, translating to $3.47B in revenue by FY28. During this period of time, I will assume for non-GAAP operating margins to expand at a similar rate as in my bull case, translating to $973M in non-GAAP operating income by FY28.

However, in this case, I will assume a lower P/E ratio of 19, which is more or less at par with the average S&P 500, given the slower growth rate in its earnings. In this scenario, we arrive at a price target of $84, which represents roughly 7% upside, taking into account the -3% drop in share price after hours.

My final verdict and conclusions

While both scenarios offer potential upside from current levels, I am leaning towards staying on the sidelines with Okta, at least until we see an inflection in revenue growth. One may claim that Okta stock is significantly undervalued compared to most other cybersecurity names. However, we do not see it as a value play anymore. More as a value trap instead, especially given its unimpressive guidance and at a time when investors are favoring companies that are growing at a much faster pace while remaining financially disciplined.

data.title: Wendy's: Beating Low Expectations Wasn't Enough, Turnaround Is A 'Show Me' Story
data.description:
Introduction

The Wendy's Company (WEN) Q3 numbers cleared a low bar as U.S. comps fell less than feared and international comps kept growing. On the earnings call, management rolled out the long-awaited “Project Fresh” turnaround roadmap, yet the stock drifted lower and quickly set new lows. The plan to close hundreds of weak U.S. stores, stop low-return domestic builds, and put capital into remodels and premium positioning seems reasonable. However, where I’m cautious is how Wendy’s competes to gain share of a consumer who seeks more value at lower prices with a customer base that’s already price-sensitive. Compounding this problem is that the balance sheet has a lot of leverage with what I see as years of deferred restaurant investment now coming due. Trading at half the sector P/E and EV/EBITDA multiple with a 6.8% yield, Wendy’s looks statistically inexpensive but the discount reflects real operational and financial gaps vs. better-run peers.

Wendy’s Q3 Breakdown

Expectations were low heading into Q3 and that’s why even with low estimates (which Wendy’s ended up beating on both the top and bottom line), the stock continued to drift lower following the results, making fresh 52-week lows.

Total revenues came in at $549.5 million, down 3.0% compared to last year but ahead of estimates by $15 million. Most of this was driven by the U.S. business that fell 3.6% while the International business at 7% of revenue actually increased 6.2%. In the U.S. business, U.S. same-restaurant sales and reduced franchise royalty and rental income outweighed modest growth in franchise fees while international expansion abroad led system-wide sales growth and new unit openings.

In aggregate, Wendy's global system-wide sales declined 2.6% on a constant currency basis mostly due to a 4.7% drop in U.S. same-restaurant sales which came in ahead of expectations offset somewhat by 8.6% same-store sales growth internationally.

On the margin side, total company-operated restaurant margins stood at 12.4%, down 270bps year over year while U.S. margins were 13.1%, reflecting a 250bps contraction mainly from inflation in beef and labor costs alongside reduced traffic though mitigated by higher average checks and better labor productivity from lower turnover and enhanced training. Adjusted EBITDA rose 2.1% to $138 million, surpassing consensus of $124 million as the company benefited from reduced incremental advertising spend and general and administrative expenses down $6.4 million and $4.9 million, respectively (Source: Bloomberg).

On the balance sheet, Wendy’s ended the quarter with $326 million in cash, and a net leverage ratio steady at 4.5x free cash flow year-to-date is clocking in just shy of $196 million. While that’s enabled some share repurchases and dividends, the balance sheet has become more and more of a problem as the company’s EBITDA has collapsed over the past two years.

In terms of some highlights from the quarter, Wendy’s announced the launch of ‘Project Fresh’, which is a turnaround plan centered on brand revitalization, operational excellence, system optimization, and capital allocation aimed at boosting U.S. average unit volumes and profitability. The company opened 172 new restaurants globally with international momentum strong, including first locations in Ireland and a record-opening day in Australia, plus new development agreements for over 320 units year-to-date with about 50 in Central Mexico.

The Strategy Could Pay Off, But There Are Risks

Looking ahead, Wendy's is betting big on Project Fresh to turn around the U.S. business. The plan rests on a few pillars: brand revitalization, operational excellence, system optimization and capital allocation. Essentially, this can be summarized as telling customers again why fresh never-frozen beef and real ingredients are worth paying for, fixing day-to-day operations through better training and tech, closing weak stores (management thinks roughly 5% of U.S. units, or about 300 locations, will go away by the end of 2026), and moving capital away from building new U.S. restaurants toward things that actually lift sales at existing ones.

On paper, it makes sense to me. Just get rid of the dogs, reinvest in the winners, and remind people why Wendy's used to feel ‘special’. I like the logic, but I’m skeptical it will work in practice right now. Almost every other chain is screaming value with $5 and $6 meals while Wendy's keeps talking premium quality. When more than half your customers make less than $75k a year, “worth it” is a tough sell against a $5 ‘Biggie Bag’ competitor across the street. In my view, without a solid answer on everyday price, it’s very possible that traffic could stay negative longer than management expects, and those hoped-for AUV gains will be harder to get.

The balance sheet also gives me some pause. If we include capital leases (which I think one should when evaluating retail chains and restaurants), net leverage is headed toward 5.8x by year-end, which feels high for a company that isn’t growing EBITDA much. The equity selloff may have been dramatic, but on an enterprise value basis, it's only down 22.3% compared to last year’s EV of $7.0 billion, shown below (Source: Bloomberg).

There’s also another problem that wasn't addressed by management on the earnings call. When we look at the income statement and cash flow statements, depreciation has been running at 1.5-2x capex for years now ($106.1 million of capex versus $145.2 million of depreciation for the LTM period).

‘Who cares?’ you ask. Well, it’s essentially the opposite problem raised by Michael Burry with depreciation for companies in the AI space, who’ve been investing way more in capex which hasn’t yet fully reflected the economic realities of these businesses’ earnings. In the case of Wendy’s, because the company hasn’t historically helped franchisees much to remodel the roughly 1,650 stores where it collects rent, there’s going to be a major cash outlay of capex now going forward. These capex spends are things that companies like McDonald's Corporation (MCD) and others do routinely, and Wendy’s lack of spending on their stores has left a lot of locations looking tired and underinvested.

While I think it’s certainly possible that management could fix some of this with sale-leasebacks on the company-owned pieces of land they still sit on and raise a few hundred million of cash, it ultimately doesn’t solve the problem as it simply trades lower debt today for higher rent tomorrow. Interest expense is already eating a chunk of cash flow, and with over $2 billion of fixed-rate debt maturing between 2028 and 2032, any rise in rates would hurt. Free cash flow remains strong (they still convert more than 100% of net income), but a lot of it is spoken for by dividends, buybacks, and now the turnaround spending.

Yet a balanced perspective has to admit this could actually work. International is legitimately doing very well (even if it is less than a tenth of revenue) with new markets opening with record days and a pipeline of more than 320 signed deals. That alone gives the stock a growth engine the U.S.-based business currently lacks. Company-operated stores are already proving that the operational playbook can move the needle. Management noted on the conference call post-quarter that they did beat system sales by over 400bps last quarter by making some of these small changes. If they can roll those same changes out to franchisees and pair them with a fresher-looking box (more remodels funded by the capital they’re pulling from new builds), I can see AUVs starting to climb again.

Cheap, but that’s justified

Wendy’s is a polarizing stock for the sellside. There are six ‘buy’ ratings, 18 ‘hold’ ratings, and five ‘sell’ ratings. In aggregate, the average price target sits at $10.02, implying about 18.0% upside not including the dividend of $0.56 which equates to a yield of 6.8% (Source: Bloomberg).

Speaking of the dividend, I think that even with the new payout ratio of 64% (having previously cut the dividend from 25 cents to 14 cents per quarter) it doesn’t give the company a lot of flexibility. Rather, it’s possible another cut or, at the very least, a multi-year freeze is coming once the turnaround spending really ramps up and cash is needed for remodels, technology, and franchisee support. Management has made returning capital a priority, but with leverage already high, I would much rather see cash go towards cleaning up the balance sheet and improving the financial profile of the business before spitting off cash to shareholders. That’s something I want to see for companies that don’t have growth needs and already have great balance sheets.

Where I’ll acknowledge the bull thesis, outside of perhaps the turnaround plan working faster and less costly than expected, is that Wendy’s is trading really cheaply. Compared to the rest of the restaurant and fast-food complex, the company trades at half the valuation on a P/E basis at 9.6x (compared to peers at 18.9x) and a near 4-turn discount on EV/EBITDA at 9.0x (compared to peers at 13.6x). Overall, I think this discount is warranted because many of Wendy’s peers own a significantly larger portion of their real estate, have more company-owned stores, and are simply better operators who are growing. For me, I’d much rather own something like a Restaurant Brands International Inc. (QSR) and play for a turnaround with the Burger King franchise since you have an experienced operator at the helm who oversaw the terrific success at Domino's Pizza, Inc. (DPZ) at his time.

Bottom line

Ultimately, I think Wendy’s turnaround plan is rational, and the international business is performing well as a growth engine, but the margin of error here is thin. Leverage is stretched, today’s consumer is obsessed with value, and closing 5% of the U.S. base will create a short-term sales drag before any reinvestment payoff shows up. I believe Wendy's can get back to modest growth eventually, but I wouldn’t bet on it being quick or painless. The stock already trades at one of the cheapest multiples in the restaurant space for a reason. So while plenty of risk is priced in, the upside still rests on proof that “quality over price” can win again in 2026. For now, I rate the stock a ‘sell’.

data.title: Kaiser Aluminum: Good Performance, Still A Buy After Q3 '25
data.description:
I believe it is a fair assessment to say that the market is currently significantly overvalued, nor do I seem to be the only person or analyst to make that particular assessment. This state of significant overvaluation has a number of implications for a value-oriented investor such as myself.

First, I don't want to hold good in-profit positions if there is a chance that significant profits may be erased - unless there is a good reason for keeping them. This is the reason I am now over 35% cash for the first time in almost 10 years. Typically, I'd want to be in as much "in the market" as possible and weather any storms that may come. For the vast majority of my positions, this is still the case - but I am more cognizant these days about taking profit. If you're a value-oriented investor, I believe there is value in considering a similar approach.

Secondly, I believe that the risk for a near-term downturn is significant. No one is clairvoyant. I am fully aware that the market may indeed continue upward for another month, another year, heck, another 10 years. If that's the case, my 65% in-market still ensures eventually good returns. But on the off-chance that the crash is closer (again, which I believe), then here's the reason for why I am much more careful with both reinvestments and holding stocks than I have been in a long time.

There's a reason I'm saying this for Kaiser Aluminum Corporation (KALU) as well. KALU stock is up almost 25% in less than 7 months, which comes to a very decent annualized rate of return. In my last article, found here, I moved the company to a Buy.

The question is, how does that rating hold itself when the share price is close to triple digits?

Kaiser Aluminum - Good business model, tricky fundamentals, and volatile commodity market

Kaiser Aluminum is, mildly put, not a well-covered company. The business is not currently considered "covered" by SA, given the length of over half a year since my last article, which is also the last on KALU. Wall Street and Quant currently have the business a Buy or Strong Buy given solid fundamentals and good upside. With over 70 years under its belt, the company's history and place in the market are solid.

It has withstood significant downturns and is likely to withstand significantly more if need be. My previous positives cited for purchase included a good valuation, recent results being mostly on track in terms of stated goals and targets, including ESG ones, and expecting double-digit EBITDA growth for the year. The transformative journey currently happening to the company is likely to, in my view, drive things higher. I've even gone so far as to give the company's leverage a lesser weight due to the growth potential I see here, and I don't consider KALU to be "speculative" for that either.

Recent results have confirmed my upside and expectations for the business.

The company is working on its efficiency program to focus its production and asset base, now found in 13 facilities across the NA region. This is a 100% NA company, with almost the entirety of NA sales. This goes to show you that neither a 100% European nor a North American company prevents me from "going there." I will take value and upside where I find it, be it in South America, in the U.S., wherever.

The company has focused its improvement on more premium markets with higher entry barriers, such as aerospace applications, increasing manufacturing efficiency, and operating leverage.

It's fair to say that the company is still somewhat commoditized. I wouldn't go so far as to say that construction or industrial ("Can stock") is anything but a commoditized market, but the Aerospace and GE, especially, are interesting. The company split these days is more interesting, and one of my reasons for investment. It's one of the lowest automotive-exposed Aluminum industries out there (compare Norsk Hydro (NHYDY) at only 8%, with most of the company's exposure being Aerospace, GE, and Packaging. While packaging is, of course, commoditized, it's not going anywhere, and this is where the efficiency comes in as well.

How much of these forecasted trends can we see in the latest Q3 '25 results?

The quarter was strong - 4th consecutive period of the company being ahead of forecasts, and once again raising full-year estimates. This goes well with the significant bounce on earnings day back in October. Even negatives like a decline in aerospace shipments due to an outage couldn't weigh down the company's positives, because demand for the company's high-strength applications has recovered, meaning the company will likely catch up here. GE was up, and other segments were stable or up as well, and on a net income basis, the company more than tripled its YoY level of income - very solid overall.

What's even more important to me is the progress the company has made on its fundamentals and balance sheet. One of my earlier cited risks for KALU has been its leverage. While the current improvement, down to 3.6x, is also due to the company's good results, it nonetheless marks a very solid improvement, especially given a successful extension of the $500M+ revolver. I'm not yet ready to remove the debt/leverage as a risk from this company, but 3.6x now, compared to about 4.5x+ about a year ago, is very solid.

The company's growth projects also remain on track, which is yet another "box checked" here.

Really, the only issues or questions here are related to that one-off shipment decline in Aerospace (which was mostly outage, there's recovery there), and how the company is prioritizing value-add, GE production, and commoditized production. How much of product A with better margins relative to B with more sales but a lower margin? What's clear to me is that in any scenario, an EBITDA growth of 3-4% is likely here.

What's more, and to sort of finalize the earnings impacts here, the company is a U.S.-based producer and source of aluminum. That means that in terms of tariff impacts, it's getting more and clearer that this is a net positive for the business. There's good demand for products across the board here.

Let's look at changes in valuation.

Valuation for Kaiser Aluminum - Upside possible, but more uncertain

We've now reached a stage for the company valuation that is in my view, complex to say the least. The company is trading at around 17.15x P/E normalized, with a yield of 3.19% which is, all things considered here, well-covered from earnings, especially now. However, in my last article, I gave the company no higher than an $80/share price target, or PT.

I'm going to be increasing that here. So what exactly has changed?

The company's growth estimates are now significantly higher than before. All you need to do is look at the estimates in my previous article. While even with those estimates, there would have been upside here, that upside would not have been as high as we're looking at here (even with a lower valuation). Due to tariffs and other factors, the company's sales are expected to grow on average over 23% the coming years, and on a 20-year normalized average, the current valuation forecast implies a fair value of nearly $200/share and an upside of 115%.

Great news, right? I must have bought more straight away?

Not quite. It continues to baffle me how often analysts seem to forget the inherent volatility of certain segments. While true that you can never "see" the downturns coming, you can use KPI's and historical records to see what the risks could be. It's why, overall, I've been so careful with KALU, and also why I've had such success investing as well as not investing in the company.

KALU has missed targets negatively, almost notoriously often.

It's fair to say that more than 7 times out of 10 for the past 10 years, the company has negatively missed targets. This goes beyond coincidence and shows us the volatility of underlying trends here. If you also look at historical earnings, you can see what happens to the company when the market turns - earnings can even go negative, as they did in 2022.

So, while I believe that earnings are going up, and I even believe some of the positives forecasted here, and am increasing my fair value target for KALU to $125/share. I spoke in my last article about the potential for a more bullish scenario. This seems to have materialized at this time, and I am acting accordingly. My new PT is $100/share. This maintains the Buy rating, if only barely.

I also would not go beyond this Buy rating and target at this time. Other analyst targets for KALU go from a low of $105 to a high of $108, but it should be noted first that there are only 2 analysts following KALU (as I said, the company seems to be underfollowed), and secondly, there was a target for KALU of $55/share less than a year ago. I would once again point you to how volatile this company can potentially be.

For investment alternatives with a safer upside in the same sector, with better fundamentals, I point you to Hydro.

Norsk Hydro is Norwegian, in aluminum. It's decently volatile, but it has a BBB rating, significantly lower debt, a similar yield, and more often beats targets or hits them than misses them. At a lower P/E multiple similar to KALU's current one, Hydro currently gives a significantly positive upside.

This is why, as things stand, Norsk Hydro is my current investment choice in Aluminum, despite KALU being a Buy here. It gives you an insight into my thing as a valuation-oriented investor.

Risks to this thesis are, in turn, as follows.

Risks To My Thesis

Aside from forecast inaccuracy and failure for the company over time, I believe the company's main risk is exposure to incredibly volatile end markets, which remain so in Q3 '25 despite some overall upside from tariffs and earnings.

The company's upside is based on this, which means that I view the realistic upside to fluctuate between 15–20% per year and 40–50% per year. Add to that the company's leverage, which is the equivalent of a millstone around the company's neck, compared to Norsk Hydro, which is solid BBB rated, IG with very little debt of sub-18% % long-term debt to capital, and on a relative comparison, you can see that there's volatility here.

As long as you're okay with that volatility, I don't see any issue investing here; however, given that you also don't want alternatives.

Thesis

  • Kaiser Aluminum is a not-uninteresting aluminum company active in 4 appealing end markets. It has a solid yield, not a solid credit (but hopefully improving over the next few years), and difficult, volatile forecasts. In order to make sure that you have a good upside here, you need to buy the company towards the cheaper end of its usual spectrum, upgraded for 2025–2027.
  • I still view this as being around $95/share for the PT, which represents an 18%+ increase for my 2024 PT, but is justified given significantly better results here.
  • I am therefore maintaining my rating for Kaiser Aluminum at a Buy with an upside of at least 15–20%, but with a potential for 25% in a bullish scenario. Volatility is "real" here, and I view peers as better alternatives – but KALU is still a Buy.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of $95/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Buy rating as of December 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dollar Tree Is Back, More Upside Likely
data.description:
Dollar Tree, Inc. (DLTR) stock just put out another strong quarter. This has been a very mixed year. First, it had to contend with the major threat of tariffs to margin power and has undergone a major move to raise prices in its "more than a $1.25" section of Dollar Tree stores. Anecdotally, a visit to a local Dollar Tree store in New York revealed that now more products than ever are above that $1.25 mark. This offsets tariff costs and increases margins. Then, of course, there was the big move to cut the fat and divest the Family Dollar brand for $1 billion. Recall, this was a stock we highlighted in the spring at BAD BEAT Investing for lopsided risk-reward.

Solid trading gains were had, then shares took a deep dive again later in the year. Now, they are on the comeback. With this quarter, the company is back, and the stock likely has upside. Shares will need to break above $120 for a confirmed breakout. Provided the market holds up, we think the stock is going to challenge that level and may break through. Let us discuss the just-reported performance.

First, the Q3 sales. Sales surpassed estimates by $60 million. Sales increased 9.4% to $4.75 billion versus last year. Keep in mind, this is comparing only the Dollar Tree comps as Family Dollar operations were present in the past. This came on the back of same-store sales spiking 4.2% as well.

Comparable same-store sales are a key figure we follow for all retailers. With comparable sales very positive, we have a good sign here despite a shaky economy and pressure on the low-end consumer. We believe the demand for lower-cost items among cash-strapped consumers is set to increase going forward, and the company's pricing strategy is paying off. That said, there was a 0.3% decline in traffic but a 4.5% increase in average ticket.

Let's turn to earnings power. This is where we think the Street is getting excited to continue bidding up the stock. Gross profit increased 10.8% to $1.7 billion, while gross margin increased 40 basis points to 35.8%. We like expanding margins. Further, factoring in expenses, for Q3, overall adjusted operating income was up 4.1% to $345.3 million. It was not all positive, however, as adjusted operating margin contracted 30 basis points to 7.3%. But it was enough for a sizable EPS beat. As adjusted, EPS was $1.21, a notable $0.13 beat versus estimates. And year-to-date, the company is delivering. Through the first three quarters, sales are up 11.0% to $13.9 billion. Same-store sales are up a very strong 5.4%, driven by a 1.8% increase in traffic and a 3.6% increase in average ticket. On top of that, gross profit has increased 11.8% to $4.9 billion, and gross margin increased by 30 basis points to 35.3%. It has been a good year. Dollar Tree is back.

Looking ahead

This was another strong quarter. DLTR is purchasing shares as well. The company repurchased 4.1 million shares for $399.0 million in Q3. Here in Q4 so far, it has purchased an additional 1.7 million shares for $176 million. As of November 1, 2025, the company still had $2.0 billion remaining under the $2.5 billion repurchase authorization. Balance sheet-wise it has $594.8 million of cash and cash equivalents, $620.0 million of commercial paper notes outstanding, and no borrowings under its credit facilities.

Finally, management refined its full-year fiscal 2025 revenue forecast to $19.35 billion to $19.45 billion. Previously comparable sales were seen at 4% to 6%, and that has been upped to 5.0% to 5.5%, hence raising the midpoint of the guide. The full-year EPS was revised from $5.32 to $5.72 up to $5.60 to $5.80. The latter point is the most bullish. Here at $111 per share, we are at 19.6X FWD 2025 numbers, which is below a market multiple. Of course, this is modest year-over-year growth, but early estimates call for 15-20% EPS gains in 2026. All things considered, we like the potential upside, especially if shares can break above the $120 level. Market weakness that sends shares lower should be considered a buying opportunity.

data.title: DBJP: JPY And USD Face Similar Issues, Limiting Hedge Value
data.description:
The Xtrackers MSCI Japan Hedged Equity ETF (DBJP) is a yen-hedged broad index tracker for the Japanese market.

Our position is that the USD and the JPY exchange rate hasn't moved much and that there are reasons why a relative parity between the two quite beleaguered currencies might continue. Therefore, the value of the hedge in the DBJP is limited. With expense ratios at 0.45% versus as low as 0.12% for other options to get exposure to the Japanese market but without the hedge, we'd opt for the latter—particularly as the hedging costs may not even be reflected in that expense ratio figure and may simply come out of the NAV performance depending on the varying cost incurred by the fund in hedging the yen. In terms of the non-FX considerations, we'd also be cautious about Japan for the time being when taking exposure to the broad index.

DBJP Breakdown

The DBJP has underlying equity exposures much like any other Japanese indexed ETF. Around 4% Toyota (TM), and overwhelming exposure to industrials and consumer discretionary being dominated by automotive stocks, including automotive parts. The Japanese index tends to perform well when the yen is doing badly, even in terms of foreign currencies like the USD, because of the export-led nature of the Japanese economy. Large cap is large cap in Japan precisely because of access to export markets, where Japan produces competitively. The Japanese index also tends to be vertically integrated domestically, meaning when the yen declines, services and labor costs, which are yen-denominated, become cheaper relative to the average selling price of a vehicle, denominated substantially in foreign currencies and sold in foreign markets. Ordinarily, the yen hedge in the DBJP has appeal on account of the fact that the negative direct offset of a weaker yen doesn't have to be felt while the underlying index climbs in yen terms on account of better terms of trade offered by a weaker yen.

The reason we are discussing the issue of exchange rate at all for the yen is the recent developments in Japan's political landscape.

  • A new administration in Japan is pushing an old Abenomics routine that is fiscally demanding. Japan has immense debt, with a massive market, including domestically, for Japanese debt despite the perennially low rates.
  • The issue is that the BoJ, which may or may not be independent in practice—this is a bit of a point of contention because they typically cooperate with governments despite being formally independent—is trying to foster an underlying level of inflation driven by wage gains from union negotiations and consequent corporate comfort with pricing action, not traditionally utilized by businesses in Japan. The point is then to be able to slowly raise rates to a higher level and avoid a currency crisis triggered by things like carry trades between JPY and other higher-rate currencies.
  • A higher rate is not compatible with greater fiscal spending when Japan already has a massive debt pile. It's not expensive at the moment, but rate hikes make it so that this time it's a different environment in which Abenomics may not be appropriate.

While a weaker JPY is causing some cost-push imported inflation, with Japan, for example, dependent on foreign, USD-denominated energy, wage growth is still not that high yet. Therefore, we aren't sure that the environment is anyway still right for rate hikes. But should the need to control inflation eventually arise, Japan has two contradicting policy objectives between the monetary authority and the state administration.

Bottom Line

While the JPY has shown some weakness on the back of the market recognizing these opposing objectives, the USD also has some of the same issues. The administration is taking actions that may erode the utility of the USD as a reserve currency through its trade policy, which is also inflationary, while also wanting to lower rates. This isn't good for the dollar, as two reasons to buy, hold, or use dollars have been affected. Therefore, we aren't sure we need the higher expense ratio and higher hedging cost of Japan ETFs right now. Furthermore, with major US end markets and concerns about things like employment, we are also not convinced of exposures to cyclical stocks within industrials and consumer discretionary. A pass.
